title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. Students will explore the traditional Ngāti Kahungunu practices of caring for this awa as a source of physical and spiritual wellbeing, connecting the health of our waterway to the health of our community.
learningObjectives: Identify and describe at least three traditional Māori practices for maintaining the health of waterways and explain their modern relevance
Conduct basic water quality tests using scientific tools and compare findings with traditional observation methods
Create a personal kaitiakitanga plan outlining specific actions to protect and enhance Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Collaborate effectively in small groups to develop and present a community awareness campaign about stream health

activities.title: Whakapapa of the Awa: Connecting to Our Waterway
activities.description: Students begin at the stream's edge, listening to local kaumātua share the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. They learn traditional methods of assessing water health through observation of insect life, water clarity, and plant growth, recording observations in their kaitiaki journals.

activities.title: Mātauranga Māori Meets Modern Science
activities.description: Working with our local environmental ranger, students use water testing kits to measure pH, nitrate levels, and turbidity. They compare these scientific measurements with traditional indicators learned from kaumātua, discussing how both knowledge systems contribute to understanding stream health.

activities.title: Rongoā Planting Day: Healing Our Awa
activities.description: Students participate in planting native rongoā plants along the stream banks, learning about how these plants both stabilize the banks and provide traditional medicines. They document the planting process and create informational tags in both Te Reo Māori and English.

activities.title: Kaitiaki Action Planning
activities.description: In small groups, students develop specific action plans to address one identified issue affecting Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Plans must incorporate both traditional knowledge and modern approaches, and include measurable goals for improvement.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu with knowledge of traditional stream care practices
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water testing kits and monitoring guides
'Te Mātauranga o Ngā Wai Māori' resource from Landcare Research
Native plants from Tūtira Native Nursery for riparian planting

tasks: Kaitiaki Journal documenting observations, test results, and reflections
Group presentation of action plan with specific, measurable goals
Practical demonstration of water testing and traditional assessment methods
Written explanation of how their plan honors both mātauranga Māori and modern science

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A3 - Safety management; C1 - Personal identity and self-worth; C2 - Relationships with other people; C3 - Healthy communities and environments
Science: Nature of Science - Participating and contributing; Living World - Ecology
Social Sciences: Continuity and Change; Place and Environment
